title: Taiko Bridge SGX Enclave Key Leak Exploit

estimatedLoss.note: Total estimated ~$1.7M based on Defi Nerd's L1 reconstruction. ETH price assumed ~$3,770 at time of incident. The per-asset convertedUSD values are approximate and their sum (~$1.83M) exceeds the rounded $1.7M total. The $1.7M figure is the consensus estimate from PeckShield, Phalcon, QuillAudits, and GoPlus; individual token USD conversions at exact attack-time prices would refine the sum.

attackTime.note: Setup/proof batch tx at 19:03:59 UTC (block 25367938). L1 asset releases from 22:07:23 to 22:28:11 UTC (blocks 25368853-25368955). Taiko official security notice at 2026-06-22T00:49:14Z.

transactions.description: Setup/proof batch: attacker EOA called executor via execute(bytes) [selector 0x09c5eabe]. Executor called SgxVerifier.registerInstance() [selector 0xa91951a2] twice, registering malicious SGX instances (IDs 5, 6). SignalService.saveCheckpoint() saved fake Taiko block 1805600 checkpoint. Bridge.processMessage() [selector 0x2035065e] called 10 times, setting 10 forged messages to RETRIABLE status.

USDC release: retryMessage() [selector 0x0432873c] released USDC from ERC20Vault to attacker EOA 0x7506.... This was the first USDC release tx; total USDC stolen across all drain txs was 675,761.236201.
ETH release: processMessage() released 130 ETH from Bridge to attacker EOA 0x7506...
Major drain transaction (block 25368955, 22:28:11 UTC). processMessage() [selector 0x2035065e] called on Bridge, releasing additional assets to attacker. Verified on-chain: status=0x1 (SUCCESS).

attackers.description: Main attacker EOA. Called executor contract, received stolen USDC/ETH/other assets. Transferred 1,990,000 TAIKO to MEXC exchange. Also held ~870 ETH (~$1.52M) at time of reports.
Second attacker wallet identified by QuillAudits. Received stolen funds alongside main EOA.
Executor contract called by attacker EOA via execute(bytes). Orchestrated SGX instance registration, checkpoint saving, and processMessage calls in a single setup transaction.

victims.description: Taiko Bridge Proxy on Ethereum L1. Implementation: 0x2705b12a971da766a3f9321a743d61cead67da2f. Released 130 ETH and other assets to attacker via forged processMessage/retryMessage calls.
ERC20Vault Proxy on Ethereum L1. Implementation: 0xb20c8ffc2dd49596508d262b6e8b6817e9790e63. Released USDC, USDT, crvUSD, WETH, WBTC, weETH, CRV, iZi to attacker via forged retryMessage calls.

description:
On June 21, 2026, Taiko's bridge infrastructure on Ethereum was exploited for approximately $1.7M. The attacker leveraged a leaked RSA-3072 private key (enclave-key.pem) that had been committed to the public taikoxyz/raiko GitHub repository on a hotfix branch. This key was used to sign all of Taiko's SGX enclaves and served as the entire trust root for the protocol's L2 state verification mechanism.

The attack unfolded in two phases. In the setup phase (block 25367938, 19:03:59 UTC), the attacker EOA 0x7506DeA0c38ca0B55364B22424374c5A1ae1B76a called an executor contract 0xe0df6fc36deb38dd11dc53d327475c2b2b0ab98a, which: (1) registered two malicious SGX instances via SgxVerifier.registerInstance() with instance keys 0x4777a54ff81357c966d7d98eea88f0f54d969135 and 0x292468e78605eca39071165b5eac4c9707ba9813 — these passed DCAP verification because the attacker's enclave was signed with the leaked key whose MrSigner matched the on-chain trustedUserMrSigner; (2) saved a fabricated Taiko L2 block checkpoint (block 1805600) via SignalService.saveCheckpoint(); (3) called Bridge.processMessage() 10 times, each delegating to SignalService.proveSignalReceived() for source chain 167000 (Taiko), which accepted the forged proofs and set 10 bridge messages to RETRIABLE status. INSTANCE_VALIDITY_DELAY was set to 0, so the freshly registered SGX instances were immediately usable.

In the drain phase (blocks 25368853-25368908, 22:07:23-22:18:23 UTC), the attacker used retryMessage() and processMessage() to release canonical L1 assets from the Bridge (0xd60247c6848B7Ca29eDdF63AA924E53dB6Ddd8EC) and ERC20Vault (0x996282cA11E5DEb6B5D122CC3B9A1FcAAD4415Ab) to attacker wallets. No corresponding MessageSent events existed on Taiko's actual L2 — the bridge messages were entirely fabricated.

Assets stolen included 675,761 USDC, 138,139 USDT, 156,832 crvUSD, 130 ETH, 20.7 WETH, 0.43 WBTC, 0.53 weETH, 126,160 CRV, 2,140,403 iZi, and 1,990,000 TAIKO. The attacker transferred 1,990,000 TAIKO (~$189K) to MEXC exchange and also used Tornado Cash for fund laundering.

Taiko responded by pausing the L1 Bridge and ERC20Vault, halting block production, and coordinating with MEXC to suspend TAIKO deposits. The team later replenished the bridge 1:1 from its own resources so no user lost funds. The chain was back online by June 30, 2026, with the bridge fully backed again.
rootCause: The root cause was an operational key management failure: the RSA-3072 private key used to sign all Taiko SGX enclaves (enclave-key.pem) was committed to the public taikoxyz/raiko GitHub repository on a hotfix branch. This key functioned as the protocol's entire trust root for L2 state verification — its MrSigner value (SHA-256 of the little-endian RSA modulus) was stored on-chain as trustedUserMrSigner. Once the key was public, nothing distinguished a legitimate Raiko-built enclave from an attacker-built enclave signed with the same key. Two compounding contract-level design choices amplified the damage: (1) INSTANCE_VALIDITY_DELAY was set to 0, allowing freshly registered SGX instances to be used immediately with no time delay; (2) retryMessage() trusted the RETRIABLE status set by a single prior forged attestation rather than re-verifying the proof independently, turning one successful forgery into a repeatable drain primitive.
attackVector:
1. Key extraction: Attacker pulled enclave-key.pem from the public taikoxyz/raiko GitHub repo and derived its MrSigner, confirming it matched the on-chain trustedUserMrSigner.
2. Malicious enclave signing: Attacker built a malicious SGX enclave and signed it with the leaked RSA key.
3. Prover registration: Attacker EOA 0x7506... called executor 0xe0df... via execute(bytes) [selector 0x09c5eabe]. The executor called SgxVerifier.registerInstance() [selector 0xa91951a2] twice, registering malicious SGX instances (IDs 5 and 6) that passed DCAP verification because the enclave signature matched the trusted MrSigner. INSTANCE_VALIDITY_DELAY=0 meant they were immediately usable.
4. Checkpoint forgery: SignalService.saveCheckpoint() [selector 0xc9a0b8c8] saved a fabricated Taiko block 1805600 checkpoint with forged block hash and state root.
5. Forged message proof: Bridge.processMessage() [selector 0x2035065e] was called 10 times, each delegating to SignalService.proveSignalReceived() [selector 0x910af6ed] for source chain 167000 (Taiko). The forged checkpoint root made fabricated Taiko-origin message signals pass L1 verification, emitting MessageStatusChanged(msgHash, 1) = RETRIABLE for 10 fake bridge messages.
6. Asset drain: In subsequent transactions, retryMessage() [selector 0x0432873c] and direct processMessage() calls released L1 assets from the Bridge and ERC20Vault to attacker wallets. No corresponding MessageSent events existed on Taiko's actual L2.
lessons: Never commit private keys to any repository branch, including hotfix branches. Use pre-commit secret scanning hooks and CI/CD pipeline checks to flag .pem files and private keys before they reach any remote branch.

A single RSA key controlling MrSigner for all enclaves is a single point of total failure. Use multi-party enclave signing, key rotation, and revocation paths that do not require a full contract upgrade.
INSTANCE_VALIDITY_DELAY should not be zero. Newly registered SGX instances should have a mandatory time delay before being usable to allow for monitoring and circuit-breaker intervention.

retryMessage() should re-verify proofs independently rather than trusting a RETRIABLE status set by the same trust chain. This would have contained the attack to a single drain instead of a repeatable primitive.
Bridge release paths should have circuit breakers that detect and halt registerInstance -> saveCheckpoint -> processMessage/retryMessage sequences within the same transaction.

Consider migrating from TEE-based verification to cryptographic validity proofs (ZK proofs) that trust no enclave, no MrSigner, and no operator discipline. The attack becomes cryptographically impossible rather than operationally unlikely.

Monitor public Git repositories for accidentally committed secrets. AI-powered secret scanning can grep every commit of every public repo at machine speed — assume this is already happening to your protocol.
